perturbation.original_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement by giving the other Party thirty (30) days' prior written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement by giving the other Party ten (10) days' prior written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 10 days may conflict with California law regarding contract termination which might imply a reasonable time exceeding 10 days, depending on the circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1657 states that if no time is specified for an act to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed. Reducing the notice period to 10 days might be deemed unreasonable depending on the specific relationship and obligations outlined in the agreement, thus contradicting the implied requirement for a 'reasonable time'.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1657. If no time is specified for the performance of an act required to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed. If the act is in its nature capable of being done instantly—as, for example, if it consists in the payment of money only—it must be performed immediately upon the thing to be done being exactly ascertained. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination notice period from 30 to 10 days, which may contradict the 'reasonable time' requirement under California Civil Code § 1657, as explained in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1. The snippet states, 'If no time is specified for the performance of an act required to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed.' While the original text specified 30 days, the changed text's 10 days might be deemed unreasonable depending on the agreement, thus potentially violating the law's implied 'reasonable time' requirement, as the Perturbation Explanation suggests.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, Licensed Users shall immediately cease use of the Licensed Trade Name and Licensed Mark as soon as practicable, but no longer than thirty (30) days, after termination.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, Licensed Users shall immediately cease use of the Licensed Trade Name and Licensed Mark within five (5) days after termination.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the allowed time to cease using the licensed trade name and mark to 5 days could be considered an unreasonably short period to fully comply, especially if extensive changes are needed, creating conflict with CA law.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this section doesn't specify an exact timeframe, it focuses on preventing actions that cause likelihood of injury to business reputation or dilution of the mark. The law generally requires that former licensees cease using a licensed mark in a manner that avoids consumer confusion. A five-day window might not be sufficient to ensure orderly cessation and avoid consumer confusion depending on the circumstances of the business operations, implying a 'reasonable' timeframe longer than five days. California Business and Professions Code, Section 14340 addresses trademark dilution, where a business is given reasonable time to end trademark name in public eye.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 14340 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the time frame for ceasing the use of licensed trade names and marks from 'no longer than thirty (30) days' to 'within five (5) days'. According to the law explanation, California Business and Professions Code, Section 14340 addresses trademark dilution, where a business is given reasonable time to end trademark name in public eye. The explanation also suggests a 'reasonable' timeframe longer than five days might be necessary to ensure orderly cessation and avoid consumer confusion. The original text allows for 'as soon as practicable, but no longer than thirty (30) days' which implies that user need not take 30 days, but allows a maximum of 30 days to comply. The modification could create a legal contradiction because the five-day window might be deemed unreasonably short and could thus cause consumer confusion based on California Business and Professions Code, Section 14340 as stated in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: In the event any suit or other legal proceeding is brought for the enforcement of any of the provisions of this Agreement, the Parties hereto agree that the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover from the other party upon final judgment on the merits reasonable attorneys' fees (and sales taxes thereon, if any), including attorneys' fees for any appeal, and costs incurred in bringing such suit or proceeding.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event any suit or other legal proceeding is brought for the enforcement of any of the provisions of this Agreement, the Parties hereto agree that the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover from the other party only costs incurred in bringing such suit or proceeding.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the provision for recovering attorneys' fees could be seen as an illegal waiver of rights under California law, specifically if the agreement involves certain types of claims where attorneys' fees are mandated by statute.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1717 pertains to attorney's fees in contract actions. While it doesn't explicitly mandate attorneys' fees in every case, it provides that if a contract provides for attorney's fees to be awarded to one party, then the prevailing party (whether that specified party or not) is entitled to reasonable attorney's fees. Removing the attorney's fee provision entirely, while not a direct violation on its face, could lead to a situation where a party is unfairly prevented from recovering fees they would otherwise be entitled to under California law interpreting Section 1717, particularly if one-sided fee provisions would otherwise apply and create an implied right to fees for the other party. The change potentially conflicts with the principles and implications of section 1717, when taken in context.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1717. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1717. (a) In any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney’s fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party, then the party who is determined to be the party prevailing on the contract, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs. Where a contract provides for attorney’s fees, as set forth above, that provision shall be construed as applying to the entire contract, unless each party was represented by counsel in the negotiation and execution of the contract, and the fact of that representation is specified in the contract. Reasonable attorney’s fees shall be fixed by the court, and shall be an element of the costs of suit. Attorney’s fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract which is entered into after the effective date of this section. Any provision in any such contract which provides for a waiver of
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the provision for the prevailing party to recover attorneys' fees, which directly contradicts California Civil Code Section 1717(a), as evidenced in the provided snippet: "In any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney’s fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party...shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs." By removing the attorney's fees provision, the modified contract potentially prevents a party from recovering fees they would otherwise be entitled to under this section.
